toxicity_notes: Leaves, stems, and florets are edible and non-toxic; cultivated as major food crops. Contains glucosinolates that can cause goitrogenic effects in animals/at-risk populations with extremely high chronic consumption — not relevant in normal dietary amounts.

Roots and leaves are edible food crops; generally non-toxic in normal culinary use. Contains glucosinolates in typical brassica amounts — can contribute to goitrogenic effects only at very high chronic intake. Not a toxicity concern in ordinary diet.

Cultivated for oilseed (canola) and edible roots/tops; generally non-toxic. Glucosinolate content in modern canola cultivars is specifically bred low. Raw seeds contain erucic acid and glucosinolates at mild levels, but food-grade product is refined and safe.

Leaves and seeds are edible and widely consumed (mustard greens, mustard condiments). Seeds are higher in glucosinolates/mustard oil than many brassicas but normal culinary use is safe. Related species affinity to livestock-heavy grazing but no acute human toxicity.
Root is edible and non-toxic; a common food crop. Leaves are edible and used in salads in many cultures. Generally regarded as safe; no significant documented toxicity.

Root is edible and widely used as a condiment; grated root produces allyl isothiocyanate which gives pungency. Usually non-toxic and safe in typical culinary amounts. Skin contact with fresh root can cause irritation; large ingestion can cause GI distress — still generally safe when used as food.
Leaves are edible and a common salad green. Contains typical brassica glucosinolates but considered safe and nutritious in normal consumption. No significant toxicity in human dietary use.

Leaves and stems are edible and used as salad green; generally non-toxic. Foliage can accumulate heavy metals/waterborne pathogens from contaminated water — safety depends on water source. Generally regarded as safe in cultivated/hydroponic settings.

Leaves and seeds are edible; commonly used as garnish and salad green. Generally non-toxic in culinary amounts. Some reports of mild goitrogenic potential at very high consumption, not a concern in normal dietary use.

Seeds are used as a condiment and spice; generally non-toxic in culinary amounts. Mustard can be a skin/respiratory irritant in concentrated preparations. Seeds are eaten as food worldwide, but in very large quantities can cause GI irritation.
Not consumed; no documented significant toxicity to humans or pets. Model organism, widespread in labs. No known important hazards; generally non-toxic.

Generally non-toxic; grown as ornamental. Mild toxicity reported for some pets in some sources, but comprehensive data are sparse. No significant human toxicity documented. Treat as mostly non-toxic, with light caution around pets.
Generally non-toxic; flowers commonly used in borders/containers. ASPCA lists as non-toxic for pets and small causes minimal risk. No significant documented toxicity.
Generally non-toxic; ornamental. No significant documented toxicity to humans or pets. Grows in rocky/alkaline sites; not consumed.

Generally marginally toxic to cats/dogs (glucosinolate derivatives can cause gastrointestinal irritation). Humans eating ornamental flowers is uncommon; mild risk for pets if consumed. Documented mild toxicity to pets via ASPCA-type listings.
Generally non-toxic; widely used as groundcover. No significant documented toxicity to humans or pets.

Seeds are edible and used for Camelina oil; considered non-toxic as a foodstuff. Oil is high in omega-3 and used as culinary/nutritional oil. No significant toxicity documented; cultivated historically as edible oilseed.

Historically used as source of blue dye (indigo relative); leaves/roots mildly medicinal. Generally mildly toxic in large herbal doses, but contemporary culinary use is effectively none. Not considered acutely toxic in normal outside horticultural exposure.

Leaves historically eaten as salad green but is quite bitter; mature plants contain higher glucosinolate levels and can be toxic to livestock in quantity. Documented as mildly toxic to livestock, especially in lush/disrupted populations. Avoid for grazing; human dietary risk is low if only young leaves are used in moderation.

Generally mildly toxic in large quantities; historically used as potherb in small amounts. Young leaves have been eaten in moderation in some cultures. Not a popular food due to bitterness. Mildly caustic/tannin-rich foliage, more caution with livestock than humans.
